summary: Cronus castrates his father Uranus; his genitals fall into the sea. Aphrodite is born from the foam. Love from violence, from sea foam.
depthMeaning: Love's 'bloody' birth story — aesthetic born from violence. This myth says love is not naive — it feeds on deep wounds.
jungian: Eros — born at the meeting point of violence and creation. The tragic source of true love.
dreamMeaning: Sea foam in a dream, a woman emerging from a shell, walking on the sea — Aphrodite myth. There is an opening to love inside.
wakingMeaning: Are you opening to love? Aphrodite says: violence and love are not separate — carry with awareness.
lesson: Love is not pink — it comes from inside violence, from the depth of the wound. Carry it with honour.

name: Tiresias's Bisexual Wisdom
summary: Tiresias lived seven years as a woman, seven years as a man. He arbitrated between Hera and Zeus on 'who enjoys more' with his knowledge — Hera angered him blind, Zeus gave him sight of the future.
depthMeaning: The wisdom of the one who lived two genders is special — that is why Tiresias is the wise counsellor in all Greek tragedy. Gender as a transcendent state of consciousness.
jungian: Integration of anima and animus. Reaching bisexual consciousness.
dreamMeaning: Your gender changing in a dream, living both sides — Tiresias myth. You accept the opposite-sex energy inside.
wakingMeaning: Do you recognise opposite-sex qualities inside you? Tiresias says: this is the door of wisdom.
lesson: Bisexual sight is the deepest wisdom. Recognise the 'other' within you.

name: Dionysus's Frenzy
summary: Dionysus punishes women in cities that don't accept him — turning them into 'maenads' (his frenzied followers); wild dances, bloody rituals on the mountains. The revolt of emotion against reason.
depthMeaning: The repressed wild side erupts collectively. When Apollo (reason) is not balanced with Dionysus (ecstasy) danger arises.
jungian: Eruption of the repressed collective shadow. Not individuals but the whole group is taken.
dreamMeaning: A crowd going wild in a dream, a wild group dance on a mountain — Dionysus myth. There is repressed wild energy inside.
wakingMeaning: Are you collectively in a too-strict group? Dionysus says: a time bomb. If it isn't consciously opened it bursts.
lesson: Balance reason and ecstasy. If you suppress one the other erupts collectively.

name: Sisyphus's Happiness (Camus)
summary: Camus rereads the Sisyphus myth in 'L'Étranger'. He says we must 'imagine Sisyphus happy' as he pushes the rock to the summit endlessly. The human who creates meaning even in the absurd.
depthMeaning: Modern existentialism's fundamental rereading of myth. Life's meaning is not given — we created it. Sisyphus is the hero who chooses the absurd in the face of the absurd.
jungian: The ego's capacity to create meaning. Refusing given meaning and producing your own.
dreamMeaning: An endless task in a dream, labour against a stone, conscious repetition — Sisyphus myth. You give your own meaning to what seems meaningless.
wakingMeaning: Is the work you do meaningless? Camus says: you create the meaning, not someone else. Do it consciously — answer absurdity with absurdity.
lesson: Life doesn't come with given meaning — you create it. Even the stone you carry consciously can be poetry.

name: Hecate — The Three-Faced Goddess
summary: Hecate is a three-headed / three-faced goddess — past, present, future. Worshipped at crossroads, thresholds, night rituals. Patron of witches.
depthMeaning: Hecate is the seer of the unseen — seeing the past wound, the present bond, the future possibility at once. The dark layer of the triple goddess.
jungian: Triple time-consciousness carried at the same time. The Self standing above time.
dreamMeaning: A three-faced woman in a dream, a crossroads, a night ritual — Hecate myth. Past-present-future appear simultaneously.
wakingMeaning: When making a decision do you only look at the present? Hecate says: past and future are also there — weigh all three together.
lesson: Deciding is seeing three times at once: where you came from, where you stand, where you're going.

name: Atalanta's Race
summary: Atalanta — the unbeatable woman runner. Those who want her must race — losers die. Hippomenes wins with golden apples.
depthMeaning: Atalanta is the 'unmarried strong woman' archetype. The golden apples she is finally deceived by — she who loses to the power of so-called 'charm'.
jungian: The independent animus side losing to its own power. Charm (anima) coming from itself.
dreamMeaning: A race in a dream, a fast-running woman, shining objects — Atalanta myth. There is a balance issue between independence and bond.
wakingMeaning: Have you become too independent? Atalanta says: independence is gold but not the only way — not with 'golden apple' tricks, with conscious choice connect.
lesson: Independence is power but not the only way. Opening to a bond is not defeat — it is choice.

name: Tinkerbell — Fairy Child
summary: Peter Pan and Tinkerbell — children who never grow up — in Neverland. The modern 'Peter Pan syndrome' is used for adults who refuse to grow up.
depthMeaning: A modern myth — the 'puer aeternus' (eternal child) is important in Jung's theory. Refusal of adulthood, desire for romantic escape.
jungian: Puer aeternus archetype. Keeping the inner child alive is beautiful but refusing responsibility is dangerous.
dreamMeaning: Flying in a dream, a place like Neverland, someone always at the same age — Peter Pan myth. The child inside resists growing up.
wakingMeaning: Are you fleeing the role of being an adult? Peter says: loving childhood is beautiful but growing up is also the joy of life.
lesson: The inner child must live but adult responsibility must be shared. Balance between them is health.

name: Don Quixote and the Windmills
summary: Old knight Don Quixote attacks windmills in his head to slay giants. To those around him he is mad — in his own dream a hero.
depthMeaning: The boundary between idealism and madness is uncertain. 'Tilting at windmills' as an expression passed into modern language — meaningless struggle.
jungian: Inflation of the ego (illusion of grandeur). But also the heroism of idealism that does not give up on the world.
dreamMeaning: Fighting a great enemy in a dream, those around saying 'stop' — Don Quixote myth. You are fighting for an ideal but reality check is needed.
wakingMeaning: Are you fighting for the impossible? Don Quixote says: your ideal is valuable, but know the real enemy — is it a windmill or a giant?
lesson: Beauty in idealism but if not aligned with reality, madness. Tell the giant from the windmill.

name: Faust's Bargain
summary: Faust sells his soul to Mephistopheles for knowledge and pleasure. The end is tragic but in Goethe's version salvation — even constant seeking is enough for forgiveness.
depthMeaning: Faust is the myth of modern ambition. Is the 'soul' paid for science, success, experience? If paid, what is paid? The fundamental question of the modern industrial age.
jungian: The bargain of the ego with the Self. The soul is not paid for knowledge — knowledge that loses the soul is false.
dreamMeaning: Signing a bargain in a dream, making a deal with a dark figure — Faust myth. You may be giving 'too much' for something.
wakingMeaning: Are you giving up your spiritual wholeness for a success / knowledge? Faust says: stop. The price is heavier than you think.
lesson: No knowledge / success is worth your spiritual wholeness. Reconsider the bargain.

name: Frankenstein's Creation
summary: Young scientist Frankenstein creates a living being from corpse parts. Then frightened, he abandons it. The creature takes revenge. Tragedy of the creator-created relationship.
depthMeaning: The myth of creative responsibility. The one who creates something is responsible for it. The ancestor of modern technology ethics (AI included).
jungian: The piece pulled from the unconscious but not recognised — takes revenge as shadow. The rule 'what you create is yours'.
dreamMeaning: Creating a being in a dream and being afraid of it, something you abandoned coming back — Frankenstein myth. What you create must be owned.
wakingMeaning: Did you flee from something you created (project, relationship, child)? Frankenstein says: the more you flee the more it monsters. Own it.
lesson: What you create is bound to you — not owning it produces a tragic revenge.

name: Alice's Rabbit Hole
summary: Alice falls down a hole after a rabbit — into Wonderland. Strange rules, a world where logic is denied. In the end she wakes.
depthMeaning: Alice is the pre-modern-psychology narrative of 'the unconscious journey'. Rabbit hole — conscious descent into the unconscious. Carroll's (a mathematician) intuition before psychology.
jungian: Descent journey into the unconscious. Not logic but symbol and dream rule.
dreamMeaning: Falling through a hole in a dream, an illogical world, talking animals — Alice myth. A discovery in the unconscious.
wakingMeaning: Are you in an area where logic doesn't work? Alice says: this is the language rule of the unconscious. Focus on symbol instead of insisting on logic.
lesson: Logic doesn't work in the unconscious — symbol works. In the rabbit hole 'why' is not asked, 'what'.

name: Sherlock Holmes — Modern Sage
summary: Holmes is the detective who reaches conclusions from details. Wisdom is transmitted through Watson's eyes — trained observation. Ancestor of the modern 'clever detective' archetype.
depthMeaning: In the modern world the wise one is no longer on a mountain — in the city, reading details. Science took the shape of wisdom.
jungian: The everyday form of the modern Wise Old archetype. Wisdom through deep observation of logic.
dreamMeaning: Solving details in a dream, reaching a large truth from a small clue — Holmes archetype. An observer inside is awakening.
wakingMeaning: Is there a problem you solved with your ability to look at details? Holmes says: this talent is valuable — develop it.
lesson: Wisdom is not on a mountain — it is in details. Learn to see.

name: Scheherazade's 1001 Nights
summary: Scheherazade tells the sultan a story every night until morning — when it ends, morning has come and the sultan cannot kill her. In 1001 nights the sultan's heart softens.
depthMeaning: The myth that storytelling is life-saving. Scheherazade consciously transforms through story. The ancestor of modern 'narrative therapy'.
jungian: Transformation through storytelling. Stories told change both listener and teller.
dreamMeaning: Telling a story and softening someone's heart in a dream, speaking from night to morning — Scheherazade myth. There is a 'power of story' inside.
wakingMeaning: Can you transform a situation through a story? Scheherazade says: tell your experience. Both you and the listener will change.
lesson: Storytelling is transformative. It can save a life — try when squeezed.

name: Rumi's Meeting with Shams
summary: The Konya scholar Rumi meets the wandering dervish Shams of Tabriz. Shams turns Rumi from a scholar into a Sufi lover. 40 days of retreat, then Shams disappears — in mourning Rumi writes the Mesnevi and Divan.
depthMeaning: The myth of a meeting completely transforming a person. Shams is the outer reflection of Rumi's 'inner teacher'. His disappearance is the beginning of his integration internally.
jungian: Encounter with the Self — in another body. 'Losing' him is the process of internalisation.
dreamMeaning: Meeting a sage in a dream, a life-changing encounter, then separation and inner remembering — Rumi-Shams myth.
wakingMeaning: Did someone come into your life, change everything, then withdraw? Rumi says: he was the outer mirror of your inner teacher — now he lives inside you.
lesson: Some encounters transform but the leaving carries the good. Not losing him — but internalising him.
